past_medical_history: Right-eye keratoconus diagnosed at age 22, managed with rigid gas-permeable contact lens
Mild vitamin D deficiency noted 1 year ago, supplementation recommended but patient reports inconsistent adherence
family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed at age 55
No known family history of multiple sclerosis or demyelinating disease

social_history.occupation: Software developer, works remotely from home
social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, approximately 1-2 beers per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a ground-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Plays recreational football (soccer) twice weekly; has not played in the past 2 weeks due to visual symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent and coherent without dysarthria. Language comprehension, naming, and repetition intact. Recall 3/3 at registration and 3/3 at five minutes. Attention intact on serial sevens. Mood and affect appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Visual acuity 20/50 right eye (baseline reduced due to keratoconus), 20/30 left eye. No relative afferent pupillary defect on swinging flashlight test. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam reveals normal optic discs bilaterally without pallor or papilledema. Extraocular movements: full range in the right eye; left eye demonstrates approximately 70% of normal abduction with horizontal diplopia at end-gaze to the left. No nystagmus noted in primary position. Mild end-gaze nystagmus on leftward gaze. Facial sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in upper and lower extremities bilaterally. Upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally in all muscle groups. Lower extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally in hip flexion, knee extension, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion, and ankle plantarflexion. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch intact throughout all dermatomes. Pinprick sensation intact. The patient reports subjective tingling in the right hand and forearm that he notes has resolved by the time of examination. Vibration sense intact at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Upper extremities: 2+ biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Lower extremities: 2+ at patellar bilaterally, 2+ at Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. Hoffman sign negative bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact without dysmetria bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with normal stride length and arm swing. Tandem gait intact without difficulty. Heel and toe walking intact.
neurological_exam.additional: No Lhermitte sign. Patient reports that the right upper extremity paresthesia he experienced 2 weeks ago lasted approximately 2 days and resolved completely. No Uhthoff phenomenon noted.

patient.chief_complaint: Two weeks of horizontal double vision, worse when looking to the left
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Yilmaz is a 29-year-old right-handed software developer who presents to the neurology service after referral from his ophthalmologist for evaluation of binocular horizontal diplopia of two weeks' duration. The patient reports that approximately two weeks ago he began noticing horizontal double vision, initially intermittent but progressively more constant over the ensuing days. The diplopia is most pronounced when he looks to the left side and resolves completely with closure of either eye. He denies any associated eye pain, photophobia, or change in visual acuity beyond his baseline reduced vision in the right eye from his known keratoconus. He has not experienced any headache, facial numbness, vertigo, or hearing changes.

Notably, three days after the onset of diplopia, the patient developed a tingling and numbness sensation in his right hand and forearm. He describes this as a pins-and-needles sensation that was constant over approximately two days before spontaneously resolving completely. He did not seek medical attention for this symptom at the time, attributing it to prolonged computer use and an uncomfortable desk posture.

The patient was initially seen by his ophthalmologist, who noted reduced abduction in the left eye and referred him for further neurological workup. He denies any prior episodes of visual disturbance, limb weakness, gait difficulty, urinary symptoms, or bowel dysfunction. He has no history of trauma, recent infections, vaccinations, or febrile illness. He has not experienced any recent weight loss, night sweats, joint pain, skin rashes, or oral ulcers. His ophthalmologist had previously diagnosed right-eye keratoconus six years ago, which has been managed with a rigid gas-permeable contact lens and has been stable.

The patient reports that his general health has been good aside from the conditions noted in his past medical history. He works long hours at a computer and notes increased fatigue over the past month, which he initially attributed to work stress. He has been taking vitamin D supplements inconsistently after being told his levels were low approximately one year ago.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, along the lateral margins of the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Largest lesion measures 10 x 6 mm in the left periventricular region
findings.borders: Several lesions show ovoid morphology oriented perpendicular to the ventricular margins

findings.location: Subcortical white matter, right frontal lobe
Left juxtacortical white matter, parietal lobe

additional_observations: Total of 8 white matter lesions identified across periventricular, subcortical, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions
The pontine lesion location correlates with the clinical finding of left abduction deficit (left VI nerve fascicular involvement)
Small punctate subcortical lesion in right centrum semiovale could represent an early demyelinating plaque or a nonspecific finding
Ventricles and sulci are normal in size for patient age
No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia
mri.impression: Multiple white matter lesions in periventricular, subcortical, juxtacortical, and infratentorial (pontine) distributions. Several periventricular lesions show perpendicular orientation to the ventricular margins. A lesion in the left dorsal pons with faint partial ring enhancement is likely the symptomatic lesion correlating with the patient's left abduction deficit. The presence of one faintly enhancing and multiple non-enhancing lesions may suggest dissemination in time, though the enhancement is subtle. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Present — 2 bands unique to CSF, not found in paired serum sample

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 6 cells/uL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 42 mg/dL (reference: 15-45). Glucose: 61 mg/dL (reference: 40-70) (ratio 0.66 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). oligoclonal_bands: Present — 2 bands unique to CSF, not found in paired serum sample. IgG_index: 0.64 (reference: 0.28-0.66). IgG_synthesis_rate: 7.1 mg/day (reference: <8.0 mg/day). myelin_basic_protein: 5.2 ng/mL (reference: <4.0). cytology: No malignant cells identified. gram_stain: No organisms seen. culture: No growth at 48 hours.

output.impression: Abnormal values: P100 latency — left eye 106 ms ms (H), Interocular latency difference 8 ms ms (H).

findings.size: Spanning less than one vertebral segment

additional_observations: Single short-segment spinal cord lesion at C5-C6 involving the dorsal columns, peripherally located and occupying less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord
Conus medullaris and cauda equina normal
No disc herniation or vertebral body abnormalities
output.impression: Single short-segment T2 hyperintense lesion in the dorsal cervical cord at C5-C6 without enhancement. The lesion characteristics are typical of a demyelinating plaque — short-segment, peripherally located, and involving less than half the cord cross-section. This spinal cord lesion provides an additional CNS location for assessment of dissemination in space. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.size: Stable, largest 10 x 6 mm left periventricular

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, adjacent to the anterior horn of the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: One new enhancing periventricular lesion identified anteriorly on the right
All other previously identified lesions are stable in size and signal characteristics
Total lesion count increased from 8 to 9
output.impression: Interval development of one new enhancing periventricular lesion adjacent to the right anterior lateral ventricle with resolution of enhancement in the previously active pontine lesion. Combined with the prior study showing lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions, the full complement of McDonald 2017 criteria for MS is now unequivocally met. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: multiple sclerosis presenting as isolated sixth nerve palsy abduction deficit demyelinating

results.title: Sixth Nerve Palsy as the Presenting Sign of Multiple Sclerosis
results.key_finding: Sixth nerve palsy can be the initial manifestation of MS due to pontine demyelinating lesions affecting the abducens nucleus or fascicle. MRI brain with contrast is essential for evaluation.

results.title: Cranial Nerve Palsies in Multiple Sclerosis: Review of Clinical Presentation and Prognosis
results.key_finding: Isolated cranial nerve palsies occur in 1-5% of MS patients at presentation. The sixth nerve is the most commonly affected cranial nerve in MS. Pontine and medullary demyelinating plaques are the usual cause.

results.title: Differential diagnosis of isolated abduction palsy in young adults
results.key_finding: In young adults without vascular risk factors, demyelinating disease should be strongly considered for isolated sixth nerve palsy. Vasculopathic causes are uncommon under age 40. MRI with attention to the brainstem is recommended.

output.summary: Sixth nerve (abducens) palsy is an uncommon but recognized presentation of multiple sclerosis, typically caused by pontine demyelinating plaques affecting the abducens nucleus or its fascicle. It occurs in approximately 1-5% of MS patients at initial presentation. In young adults without vascular risk factors, demyelinating disease should be a leading diagnostic consideration for isolated sixth nerve palsy. Comprehensive MRI including brainstem sequences is essential for evaluation.

contraindications: Absolute lymphocyte count must be monitored; discontinue if sustained below 500 cells/uL
Avoid in patients with known severe active infections
warnings: Flushing and gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain) are common, especially in the first month; taking with food may reduce GI side effects
Rare risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) with prolonged severe lymphopenia
Monitor CBC with differential every 6 months
No significant interactions with cetirizine or vitamin D3 supplementation
output.formulary_status: Tier 3 specialty medication, prior authorization required
alternatives: Teriflunomide 14 mg daily (oral alternative, moderate efficacy)
Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) 30 mcg intramuscular weekly (injectable, moderate efficacy)
Glatiramer acetate 40 mg subcutaneous three times weekly (injectable)
Ocrelizumab 600 mg IV every 6 months (higher efficacy, consider if high disease activity)

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative; no longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis; brain lesion distribution typical for MS rather than NMOSD
MOG antibodies negative; pontine involvement pattern more typical of MS than MOGAD
Should be considered given diplopia presentation, but MRI lesion explains the deficit centrally; AChR and MuSK antibodies negative
Normal inflammatory markers; no restricted diffusion on MRI; lesion morphology and distribution more consistent with demyelination
No headache history; periventricular perpendicular lesion orientation atypical for migraine; enhancing pontine lesion not consistent with migraine-related changes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on cranial nerve palsy onset, associated symptoms, and any prior neurological episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subacute onset of diplopia with transient right upper extremity paresthesia occurring days later, suggesting multifocal CNS involvement

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including detailed cranial nerve and ocular motility assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left eye abduction deficit (70% of normal), mild end-gaze nystagmus; remainder of neurological exam normal with resolved sensory symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast with attention to brainstem
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in periventricular, subcortical, juxtacortical, and infratentorial (pontine) distributions with faint enhancement of the pontine lesion

optimal_actions.action: Order screening blood work including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, HbA1c, lipid panel, ESR, CRP, ANA, and vitamin B12 to exclude vascular and systemic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All routine labs normal except low vitamin D; vascular risk factors and autoimmune screening negative

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline positive oligoclonal bands (2 bands), normal IgG index, mildly elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Order MRI cervical and thoracic spine to assess for spinal cord lesions and dissemination in space
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment cervical cord lesion at C5-C6 adding another CNS location for dissemination in space

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to evaluate for subclinical optic nerve demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly prolonged P100 latency in left eye suggesting subclinical optic nerve demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Apply McDonald 2017 diagnostic criteria to assess whether MS diagnosis can be established
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dissemination in space met (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial lesions). Dissemination in time borderline — faint pontine enhancement with non-enhancing lesions may satisfy DIT; CSF oligoclonal bands (though borderline at 2) can substitute for DIT per 2017 criteria.

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone for acute relapse management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improvement in abduction deficit over days to weeks
Appropriate DMT selected based on disease activity, safety profile, and patient preferences

critical_actions: Obtain MRI brain with gadolinium to evaluate brainstem lesion correlating with abduction deficit
Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands to support MS diagnosis
Order aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Screen for vascular risk factors and thyroid disease to exclude other causes of sixth nerve palsy
Apply McDonald 2017 criteria to establish MS diagnosis
Treat acute relapse with IV corticosteroids
Initiate disease-modifying therapy to prevent future relapses
contraindicated_actions: Attributing abduction deficit solely to keratoconus-related refractive changes without neurological workup
Performing brain biopsy as initial diagnostic step without exhausting non-invasive workup
Administering live vaccines while on immunosuppressive disease-modifying therapy
Ignoring the transient paresthesia episode as irrelevant to the current presentation
Delaying disease-modifying therapy initiation without clinical justification after diagnosis is established
key_reasoning_points: Isolated sixth nerve palsy in a young adult without vascular risk factors should raise suspicion for demyelinating disease rather than microvascular ischemia
The transient right upper extremity paresthesia occurring shortly after diplopia onset suggests multifocal CNS involvement with dissemination in space clinically
MRI findings of periventricular lesions with perpendicular orientation (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, and infratentorial (pontine) lesions are highly suggestive of MS
The pontine lesion directly explains the clinical finding of left abduction deficit via involvement of the left abducens nucleus or fascicle
Borderline CSF findings (only 2 oligoclonal bands, IgG index at upper limit of normal) create moderate diagnostic uncertainty appropriate for this difficulty level
Normal vascular risk factor screening, thyroid function, and negative autoimmune markers narrow the differential toward a demyelinating etiology
Low vitamin D level is a confounding incidental finding that has epidemiological association with MS but is nonspecific
Per McDonald 2017 criteria, oligoclonal bands in CSF can substitute for dissemination in time — this is critical for making the diagnosis in this case with borderline imaging evidence of DIT

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to atypical presentation with isolated sixth nerve palsy as the presenting sign, borderline CSF findings (only 2 oligoclonal bands with normal IgG index), transient paresthesia that resolved before examination, and an incidental low vitamin D level that could represent a red herring. The agent must recognize the significance of the pontine MRI lesion correlating with the clinical deficit and integrate borderline CSF results with imaging findings to apply McDonald criteria.
